frozen_components: Pre-trained model parameters (frozen during fine-tuning, updated only via LoRA)
architecture.summary: KORE is a two-stage knowledge injection method for large multimodal models (LMMs) that combines knowledge-oriented augmentation and knowledge-oriented constraint. In stage 1, KORE-augmentation automatically expands individual knowledge items into multi-round dialogues and instruction tasks, creating structured training data. In stage 2, KORE-constraint stores previous knowledge in the covariance matrix of LMM linear layer activations, decomposes it via SVD to extract null space, and initializes LoRA adapters by projecting original weights into this null space. This constrains fine-tuning to a direction that minimally interferes with prior knowledge while enabling new knowledge injection.
trained_components: LoRA adapter matrices initialized via null-space projection
Knowledge-oriented augmentation pipeline (dialogue and instruction generation)

biblio.title: KORE: Enhancing Knowledge Injection for Large Multimodal Models via Knowledge-Oriented Controls

claims.statement: Kore (rank=235) achieves improvements in knowledge adaptation (K.A) on EVOKE, outperforming LoRA by more than twofold
Kore (rank=235) achieves improvements of in Avg score and in HARS over the best baseline
On LLaVA-v1.5 (13B), Kore surpasses LoRA across all dimensions, achieving improvements of in Avg and in HARS over Replay
On Qwen2.5-VL (7B), Kore achieves improvements of in Avg and in HARS over Replay
Ablation study: W/o Augmentation decreases knowledge adaptation (K.A) by a substantial margin
Kore-augmentation outperforms general augmentation methods for text (knowledge-aware) by in K.A, in Avg and in HARS

claims.dataset: MME, ScienceQA (reconstruction analysis); OneVision samples for covariance collection
claims.statement: Covariance matrices of linear layer inputs in LMMs capture multimodal knowledge and exhibit distinct outlier patterns for related tasks, indicating that distinct tasks exhibit different outlier distributions in the covariance matrix
Existing methods (full fine-tuning, PEFT) often lead to catastrophic forgetting of pre-existing knowledge and struggle to achieve robust generalization
